description: Halliburton is a global oilfield services and energy technology provider serving upstream operators across drilling, evaluation, completion, production, intervention, and reservoir management.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Founded in 1919, Halliburton sells products and services that help energy operators plan, drill, complete, produce from, intervene in, and eventually retire wells. Its practical domain is the full upstream asset life cycle, where customers often buy field execution, engineering expertise, downhole hardware, chemistry, and software as one tightly bundled package.

The company is not a consumer energy brand; it lives in the industrial layer where geology, capital budgets, safety requirements, and vendor lock-in meet for a long day at the rig site. That makes it a useful registry target for studying how proprietary energy infrastructure is packaged around specialized knowledge and operational data.

overview.title: Product Footprint
paragraphs: The seed products map cleanly to Halliburton's well construction and completions businesses. Drilling services include drill bits, directional drilling, drilling optimization, measurement-while-drilling, managed pressure drilling, well control, drilling fluids, cementing, and related real-time services.

Completion tools include intelligent completions, liner hangers, multilateral systems, packers, subsurface flow controls, safety valves, sand control, frac sleeves, frac plugs, perforating, wellbore service tools, completion fluids, and intervention tools. In plain terms, Halliburton helps operators get the hole drilled, keep it useful, and charge handsomely for the privilege.

overview.title: Digital And Automation Layer
paragraphs: Halliburton also operates a meaningful digital layer through offerings such as DecisionSpace 365 and LOGIX automation and remote operations. These products connect subsurface models, rig controls, remote operations, AI/ML workflows, digital twins, and real-time field data into proprietary operational systems.

The open-architecture and OSDU language around DecisionSpace is especially relevant for Free The World because it creates a boundary to inspect: where Halliburton uses open standards and customer-owned data, and where the commercial stack remains closed, bundled, and contractually protected.

overview.title: Transition Exposure
paragraphs: Although the company belongs primarily in oil and gas equipment and services, Halliburton also markets low-carbon and new-energy offerings around CCUS, geothermal, hydrogen storage and transportation, direct lithium extraction, and emissions management. These are extensions of its well planning, drilling, reservoir, monitoring, and project execution capabilities rather than a clean break from hydrocarbons.

For alternatives research, full-service open substitutes will likely be rare. Better comparison targets are narrower layers: open subsurface data standards, reservoir modeling tools, drilling analytics, industrial IoT stacks, remote monitoring systems, environmental measurement projects, and open workflows that reduce dependency on vertically bundled oilfield platforms.
